entries.title: Horoscope for Saturday, April 11, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Aquarius.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>A discussion with a friend or a member of a group could be helpful today. Certainly, it will be powerful and intense. But advice from someone older or more experienced should also be taken into consideration. After all, you don't have to reinvent the wheel.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're high-viz today, perhaps because you have strong feelings about something. (In fact, you might feel obsessed.) This could relate to some behind-the-scenes plans that you feel should be respected or observed. This is not an impulse. You're in it for the long haul.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is an excellent day to study, learn and pursue opportunities in publishing, the media, medicine and the law. You have definite ideas about what you want to achieve. It's possible someone older or more experienced will help you do this by urging you to modify your goals.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today, you might have an important discussion with a boss, parent or someone in authority. It could be about shared property, financial matters, loans, debts, credit cards or inheritances — something like that. They might want assurance about something.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Be helpful and conciliatory when dealing with spouses, partners and close friends in discussions because they might want to firm up details about higher education, travel or something to do with medicine and the law. This is a good day to get your ducks in a row.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're pulled in two directions today: Part of you wants to travel, get a change of scenery and enjoy goofing off in a pleasant way. The other part of you wants to work hard to accomplish something and to improve your health. Either way, you have the practical resources to support your choice. (Lucky you.)</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a passionate day for romantic partners. You might have definite ideas about socializing, as well as dealing with your kids. Someone older might weigh in with their opinions. Perhaps a partner feels increased responsibilities. This is a good day to plan for future fun events and vacations.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today, family discussions will be intense and very much to the point. Someone older or wiser might share their expectations as well, especially about your work, your responsibilities and perhaps even your health. Nevertheless, this is also a playful, fun-loving day. Lighten up!</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You're persuasive today, to put it mildly, because you have something to say, and you intend to be understood. As the traveler of the zodiac, you might be interested in making some long-range travel plans. Or you might have ideas for a social occasion that will be lively and competitive.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Although you are focused on restoring order at home, you might be zealous about financial issues. This might be about the cost of something. It might be about purchasing something. Or it might be about finding the funds to do what you want.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This morning you are fixated on a particular idea — dare we say obsessed? (We did.) Because of this, of course, you'll be persuasive and convincing when talking to others. But if you come on too strong, you will turn them away. Let your enthusiasm sell your ideas, not your zeal.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Behind-the-scenes research might uncover some interesting information today. It likely relates to financial matters, earnings, cash flow or maybe a previous expense. Meanwhile, enjoy the beauty of your surroundings today.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Tricia Helfer (1974) shares your birthday. You're a humanitarian with a strong sense of justice. You are optimistic, persuasive and diplomatic. This is a year of teaching and learning. Take time to renew your spiritual or religious beliefs. Explore philosophies that will give you a better sense of the true meaning of your life.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Wagers on Munetaka Murakami for Rookie of the Year make sense, even if White Sox continue to flounder
entries.summary: <p>LAS VEGAS — Blame it on the ChiSox.</p><p>That might be my excuse, should this sportsbook-app-funding experiment wind up pear-shaped.</p><p>At noon on April Fools’ Day, of all days, I settled into utter repose, scribing duties fulfilled, to watch baseball the rest of the day.</p><p>That’s when an announcer rousted me. Already, he said from some game, the White Sox were 1-5 after a 10-0 defeat in Miami, with a horrible -31 run differential.</p><p>My coconut whirred. I scanned several phone apps — none of which I’ve ever dumped a dollar into, for old-school reasons I’ve documented — to gauge the going price on the Sox finishing with the game’s fewest victories.</p><p>BetMGM had +600, or risk $100 to win $600.</p><p>I zipped west down Tropicana, parking across the street from the MGM Grand in an apartment lot behind the Oyo hotel and casino, the area’s lone free spots.</p><p>I half-hoped I’d be able make that wager at the MGM Grand sportsbook. No dice. The BetMGM app, a manager informed me, has menus produced in New Jersey independent of the MGM Grand’s book.</p><p>So I had to fund my BetMGM cell account, which I had created years ago to monitor odds. I did so painlessly, with a friendly dude who roams the MGM Grand to assist such customers.</p><p>At 2 p.m., I gave a teller cash and punched in my Sox wager at +600, which was welcome-boosted to +750.</p><p>Two hours later in a phone interview for another column, Westgate SuperBook executive consultant Jay Kornegay told me he had made that same +600 bet a few weeks earlier.</p><p>BetMGM’s figures hadn’t budged, even with a week of evidence.</p><p>Value, defined.</p><h3>Top passer</h3><p>I wasn’t finished, for BetMGM also dangled 80-1 Heisman Trophy odds on Oklahoma State quarterback Drew Mestemaker.</p><p>Thirty-to-1 had been the going rate on the 6-4, 210-pound native of Austin, Texas, who led college football with 4,379 passing yards (34 touchdowns, nine picks) last season at North Texas.</p><p>Mestemaker and Notre Dame’s CJ Carr are the game’s lone returnees who averaged an NCAA-best 9.4 yards per toss, near the double-digit figure that makes NFL scouts salivate.</p><p>He followed coach Eric Morris, and other assistants and players, to Stillwater. Those 80-1 odds represented exceptional value, so I poked it into my BetMGM app.</p><p>After I submitted it, the employee who had assisted me made his own Mestemaker wager. And 10 minutes after having chatted with Kornegay, he bet on Mestemaker, too.</p><h3>Pigskin additions</h3><p>This is a key window to tap college pigskin value, as we did here last week with John Mateer of Oklahoma, with two months before the game’s annuals are published. Punters must excavate and rate their own information.</p><p>I’m bullish on squads that beat the spread, which includes Virginia (.622 ATS last season) and Ole Miss (.615 ATS).</p><p>I grabbed long-shot title tickets on Oklahoma State (500-1) and Virginia (200-1), just in case, and 40-1 on Ole Miss, where quarterback Trinidad Chambliss will play this season.</p><p>Plus, I snagged 10-1 on LSU — because it’s imperative to have Lane Kiffin in the portfolio.</p><h3>Bleeps ’n’ giggles</h3><p>Back to baseball.</p><p>I had coat-tailed Southern California handicapper Tommy Lorenzo early, betting the Nationals under 69½ wins, which was whittled to 64½ within two weeks at other shops.</p><p>From the front office to the field, the Nats are greenhorns, so I also grabbed +500 odds on them to finish with the least amount of wins.</p><p>Then that announcer detailed the woebegone Sox. This is a franchise that has recorded three consecutive triple-digit-defeat seasons and continues slipping down that slope.</p><p>Four in a row would pit the Sox a season behind the pathetic 1938-42 Phillies, bearers of the all-time streak of five 100-loss campaigns in a row.</p><p>The next day, I wore a black Marlins cap (bearing a white logo), as a nod to their gritty 2025 season, to the William Hill shop at the M Resort to survey more value.</p><p>The Marlins topped the National League East, so I snagged 30-1 division-winning odds on it, 100-1 for a Fish-Phils 1-2 divisional finish.</p><p>I bought the Brewers at +275 to win the NL Central, since they’ve won three in a row. Plus, they led baseball in wins a year ago, so I got 40-1 that they’d repeat that feat.</p><p>For further bleeps and giggles, I took World Series fliers on Brewers over Yankees (100-1) and Yankees over Brewers (125-1).</p><h3>Murakami insurance</h3><p>I did invest in new Sox slugger Munetaka Murakami, the southpaw who smacked 56 dingers for Yakult four years ago in Japan, to win American League Rookie of the Year at +600 odds.</p><p>A 26-year-old “rookie” who played eight seasons with more than 3,500 career at-bats and six different seasons with at least 28 homers in Japan? More stellar value.</p><p>After the team’s disastrous start, however, the Sox swept the injury-riddled Blue Jays. As I file this, the Sox had seven defeats. Three teams had eight; one of those, the Giants, matched the Sox’ -27 run differential.</p><p>Should the ChiSox ultimately avoid baseball’s basement, I would wager that it will be because of Murakami’s slugging.</p><p>The insurance policy was wise, not even requiring an app.<br></p>
entries.author: Rob Miech

entries.title: Safer CTA includes more police presence
entries.summary: <p>I write regarding the recent Sun-Times story partly headlined online <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2026/04/01/chicago-police-staffing-study-larry-snelling" >“Top cop bullish on study calling for hundreds more officers.”</a> As a lifelong rider of mass transit and the parent of a daily CTA passenger, I urge that we do not overlook the need for increased police presence on the Chicago Transit Authority. </p><p>By comparison, New York City has significantly more sworn law enforcement personnel assigned per rider to its transit system than Chicago.</p><p>Importantly, the newly passed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/12/16/illinois-transit-bill-signed-pritzker" >Northern Illinois Transit Authority Act </a>calls for addressing law enforcement regionally. The CTA operates in nearly 40 suburbs, Metra maintains its own police force that requires ongoing evaluation and Pace Suburban Bus relies on local municipal police. Fortunately, the Cook County sheriff and Illinois State Police are now engaged and will carry more immediate responsibility under the act. In response to federal concerns, the CTA recently increased police hours, added sheriff’s deputies, implemented more rigorous fare-card checks and installed high-barrier gates.</p><p>Finally, safety is not solely about policing. We must continue to prioritize social services, homelessness outreach, cleanliness and Americans with Disabilities Act accessibility. The addition of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/01/15/cta-metra-pace-security-nita-law" >transit ambassadors</a> at stations — a proven model in other major cities — is included in the new transit law, effective this summer.</p><p><i>Kirk Dillard, chairman, Regional Transportation Authority</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2c0000" name="module-2c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Chicago’s housing hurdles </b></h3><p>Chicago approved the <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoispolicy.org/reports/building-not-permitted-building-permits-in-illinois-are-a-barrier-to-housing-affordability/" target="_blank" >fewest housing permits per capita</a> among the ten most populous metro areas in the country in 2023. The average wait time between application and approval exceeds <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoispolicy.org/what-does-it-take-to-get-a-building-permit-in-chicago/#:~:text=nearly%20120%2C000%20homes.-,Chicago%20lags%20behind,for%20housing%20per%20100K%20residents" target="_blank" >80 days</a>. The city is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/06/chicago-225-000-affordable-rental-units-poorest-residents-nlihc-report" target="_blank" ><u>short</u></a> 225,000 affordable rental units, and rents have climbed 35% since before the pandemic. A recent <a class="Link" href="https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/chicago-il-housing-affordability-property-taxes-crime-gun-violence-poll/" target="_blank" ><u>survey</u></a> found housing was residents' top concern — above crime and education.</p><p>All of this is connected. Chicago doesn't have an affordability problem so much as a supply problem, and its permitting record is a direct cause. When building is slow, expensive, and a legal maze, builders simply build less. Once that happens, rents climb.</p><p>Into this vacuum, some politicians have <a class="Link" href="https://www.wsiu.org/state-of-illinois/2026-02-24/illinois-legislators-introduce-bills-to-regulate-pricing-based-on-personal-data" target="_blank" >offered</a> a villain: algorithmic pricing software used by landlords to set rents. It's a satisfying story because technology, artificial intelligence and algorithms are all politically dirty words right now, but banning a pricing tool doesn't add a single unit to Chicago’s housing stock.</p><p>It doesn't shorten an 80-day permit window, and it doesn't untangle the regulatory knot that makes construction here more expensive than almost anywhere else in the country. It's the political equivalent of blaming the thermometer for the fever.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/04/05/chicago-mayor-brandon-johnson-overhaul-housing-commercial-development-process" >“Cut the Tape”</a> initiative and Gov. JB Pritzker's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/04/03/illinoisans-poll-affordable-housing-crisis-rennt-state-legislature-rich-miller" >“Missing Middle”</a> report have both <a class="Link" href="https://blockclubchicago.org/2025/07/28/why-is-it-so-expensive-to-build-affordable-housing-in-chicago/" target="_blank" ><u>identified</u></a> over 100 ways to make it easier to build housing. That's encouraging on paper. But the city's instinct still runs toward giveaways rather than getting out of the way — selling vacant <a class="Link" href="https://housingstudies.org/releases/Data-Highlighting-ETOD-Implications-Vacant-Land/#:~:text=Basic%20Facts%20about%20Vacant%20Land,in%20Chicago's%20communities%20of%20color." target="_blank" ><u>lots</u></a> for $1, offering subsidies, leaving the underlying regulatory labyrinth intact.</p><p>Cash assistance means very little if permitting delays and legal costs remain the same.</p><p>Other cities have shown what actually works. Minneapolis scrapped single-family-only zoning in 2018 and has seen <a class="Link" href="https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5347083" target="_blank" ><u>meaningfully</u></a> lower rents relative to comparable cities since. Austin added roughly 50,000 rental units between 2023 and 2024 and watched average rents fall more than 20% from their peak.</p><p>Chicago has the land, the infrastructure, and political acknowledgment that a housing crisis exists. What it lacks is the willingness to follow through. Housing policy needs more "yes" and less "no,” and right now, Chicago is still mostly saying no.</p><p><i>Elizabeth Hicks, head of external affairs, Consumer Choice Center</i></p><h3>Elgin’s rental licensing program is a success </h3><p>Concerning Chicago's proposed mandatory inspection <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/03/30/landlords-rental-inspections-trump-tsa-dhs-immigration-loyola-capitol-riots-mail-in-ballots" >program</a> for rental units, I can point out that the city of Elgin has had a rental licensing program for years, and other suburban communities have implemented similar programs as well. I have conducted thousands of rental inspections while working as a property maintenance officer.</p><p>There are many conscientious landlords who maintain their rental properties in a responsible manner, and there are some who, either through naivete or neglect, seem to feel their properties will maintain themselves.</p><p>The rental program isn't terribly intrusive, and it certainly isn't a cash grab. Some landlords came to value it as a tool, informing them of needed repairs they may not have been aware of or had been postponing. There is a fee, but there are incentives for proactively keeping up with maintenance issues that waive fees and inspections for a year or two. And of course, landlords who were reluctant to correct violations were compelled to do so.</p><p>My opinion is that Elgin's rental licensing program has been successful, mainly because of the following result: Bad landlords got better or they got out. And Elgin's housing stock improved.</p><p><i>Shawn O'Leary, Elgin</i></p><h3>Tip system works</h3><p>I’ve worked in the hospitality industry for four years, currently as a server at Bronzeville Winery. This career has given me valuable skills, flexibility and most importantly, the ability to earn a strong, reliable income.</p><p>There is a growing disconnect between what policymakers claim and what workers like me actually experience. The narrative that tipped workers aren’t making enough simply isn’t true. In Chicago, every tipped worker is legally guaranteed to make at least the full minimum wage. In reality, most of us earn well above that. This has always been the case, and claims to the contrary are misinformation that ignore how the system actually works.</p><p>Most City Council members looked at the stats and heard our voices when they voted to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/18/city-council-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants-brandon-johnson" >freeze the wage for tipped workers </a>at its current level.  Phasing out the subminimum wage for tipped workers would result in fewer hours of operation. For me and all other Chicago servers, that means fewer work hours available on the schedule. A higher mandated hourly wage doesn’t help when there aren’t enough shifts to work and doors close.</p><p>Since the “tip credit” phase-out began, support roles have been disappearing, leaving servers and managers to juggle multiple positions at once while trying to deliver excellent service. It’s exhausting and unsustainable.</p><p>Servers are telling city leaders that eliminating the "tip credit" system will reduce our opportunities and increase our workload while ultimately lowering our take-home pay. We need solutions grounded in reality. Listen to the workers who live this every day and support policies that preserve jobs, protect hours and maintain the tip credit system that actually benefits us.</p><p><i>Cierra Milsap, server, Bronzeville Winery</i></p><h3></h3><h3>Banning nicotine pouches could increase smoking</h3><p>We're three Illinoisans in our 20s to 40s who use nicotine pouches — products like ZYN, Rogue, Velo — and we’re raising the alarm about a proposed bill to <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=3148&GAID=18&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=165788&sessionid=114" target="_blank" >ban flavored tobacco</a>.</p><p>As currently drafted, the bill would ban flavored nicotine pouches in Illinois starting June 1.</p><p>While introduced as legislation that bans “flavored tobacco,” the bill goes much further and hits nicotine pouches, too.</p><p>Numerous tobacco and medical researchers — most prominently <a class="Link" href="https://www.rutgers.edu/news/nicotine-pouches-may-offer-path-reduced-tobacco-harm#:~:text=In%20first%20national%20estimate%20of,for%20Nicotine%20and%20Tobacco%20Studies" target="_blank" >Rutgers</a> University — have also found that pouches are "a possible step toward reducing or quitting more dangerous forms of nicotine delivery."</p><p>Flavors can help; most smokers who have switched to pouches say so. Pouches that taste like tobacco are understandably less appealing (would you choose a tobacco flavor for gum at the supermarket checkout?). Mint, citrus, coffee and similar flavors appeal to adults, not kids. </p><p>Yet pouches in flavors like these would be banned by the bill, even though adult consumers like us use flavored pouches to reduce risks to our health.</p><p>If flavored pouches disappear from store shelves, some people are going to go back to smoking. That's not a talking point; we’ve already seen this with data relating to flavored products in a 2021 <a class="Link" href="https://ysph.yale.edu/about-school-of-public-health/communications-public-relations/publications/public-health-magazine/article/a-flavored-vaping-ban-may-have-led-teens-to-cigarettes" target="_blank" >Yale School of Public Health study</a>.</p><p>Lawmakers should follow the science and ditch the bill before they risk making public health in Illinois worse.</p><p><i>Alexander White, Oak Forest</i><br><i>Jeff Robinson, Mount Greenwood </i><br><i>Rafal Duha, Chicago Ridge</i></p><h3>A poem for current times, war </h3><p>NOK (Next of Kin)</p><p>No one yet told their dad or mom <br>They're never, ever going home<br>High school; boot camp; deploy; bombed <br>Remains are here, they're all gone</p><p>Heard them stop in the driveway<br>Black SUV, government plates<br>Chaplain walks slowly to the door<br>She screams, faints, falls to floor</p><p>We remember first, the last to die<br>The rest forgotten, dead for lies<br>Vietnam, Iraq, Afghanistan,<br>Now die for oil, Israel, in Iran</p><p>NOK visits haunt the U.S.A.<br>New generation down the drain<br>Grief crashes like a tidal wave<br>Cold white stones; ranks, dates, and names</p><p><i>Nathan Kempthorne, Rogers Park</i></p><h3>JD Vance’s real talk </h3><p>In 2016, JD Vance <a class="Link" href="https://ohiocapitaljournal.com/2022/04/19/americas-hitler-old-j-d-vance-message-turns-up-in-heated-senate-primary/" target="_blank" >wrote</a> to his former law school roommate Josh McLaurin: “I go back and forth between thinking Trump is a cynical a—hole like Nixon who wouldn't be that bad (and might even prove useful) or that he's America's Hitler. How's that for discouraging?”</p><p>Before a ceasefire was put in place, President Donald Trump threatened that a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/07/ceasefire-brings-little-relief-from-psychological-warfare-for-chicago-residents-with-ties-to-iran" >“whole civilization will die tonight.”</a> Destroying a whole civilization is something Hitler tried to do.</p><p><i>Alan Rhine, Glenview</i></p><h3>Art of distraction</h3><p>In my opinion, Donald Trump knew in advance about Melania’s public statement denying close ties to Jeffrey Epstein. It’s even possible he — or a member of his team — was part of the crafting of her remarks, most of which I suspect were lies.</p><p>This is likely another deliberate trick of distraction to shift the focus of attention away from the president's declining popularity and cascading problems. It's the generation of more chaos and confusion which, of course, have been Trump's m.o. and rhetorical strategy.</p><p>Do we really think the White House would allow Melania to speak without any vetting? The White House knew this story would titillate the media and perhaps dominate at least one news cycle speculating about whether she was lying and if the president knew.</p><p>How ironic that other stories previously were used to distract from the Epstein Files; yet now Epstein is being used to take the focus off other problems.</p><p>I predict that, as we get closer to the November election, Trump's rhetorical strategy will be to intentionally splatter the media landscape with more absurd and irrational stories. That’s the goal.</p><p><i>Richard Cherwitz, professor emeritus, University of Texas at Austin</i></p><h3>Playing politics</h3><p>There is not a single case in which <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/07/chicago-crime-justice-department-doj-boutros-trump-midway-blitz-corruption" target="_blank" >U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros</a> doesn’t involve politics in his decision-making. Full Stop. Period. Zero. And anyone who says otherwise, is misstating reality. And anyone who states otherwise is, like Donald Trump, an armchair expert who doesn’t know what they’re talking about. Period.</p><p><i>Dave White, Rogers Park</i></p><h3>Big bucks take fun out of college sports</h3><p>It is fun to watch March Madness basketball and root for your favorite team. The specter of money, however, looms over the entire enterprise and college sports, in general. </p><p>Teams stock their rosters with players obtained through the transfer portal and by name, image and likeness, or NIL, offers. Some players play for <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/5408913/2024/04/12/college-football-transfer-portal-multiple-schools/" target="_blank" >three or four </a>schools. Some actually receive a<a class="Link" href="https://www.morningstar.com/news/marketwatch/20260124142/when-college-athletes-go-to-the-nfl-some-of-them-are-taking-pay-cuts-in-the-millions" target="_blank" > pay cut</a> when they turn pro. </p><p>Those players who do not make the big bucks could be tempted to fix games because of the numerous available gambling sites or by association with unsavory characters. </p><p>Coaches are paid millions each year and are on a rotating merry-go-round. The TV networks thrive on advertising dollars. The "student-athlete" is no more. All praise to the mighty dollar.</p><p><i>Philip S. Witt, Northbrook</i></p><h3>Say aloha to Hawaiian coffee </h3><p>I’d like reader Mike Sienkowski, who recently <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/04/06/drunken-driving-halt-act-impaired-subminimum-wage-coffee-tariffs-nurses" >wrote about coffee and tariffs</a>, to know that coffee is definitely grown in America tariff-free. Hawaii is part of the United States, and <a class="Link" href="https://perfectdailygrind.com/2022/01/a-guide-to-hawaiian-coffee-production/" target="_blank" >Hawaii grows and produces</a> the very finest coffee. While Kona coffee grown on the Big Island of Hawaii is the most famous, other Hawaiian islands, also, produce terrific coffee. If it can’t be found in a local store, it can always be ordered direct from the Hawaii coffee growers. Try it!</p><p><i>Margie Stone, Deerfield</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Segregation, racism stifle economic mobility for many in the Black community
entries.summary: <p>For nearly a decade, researchers with <a class="Link" href="https://opportunityinsights.org/" target="_blank" >Opportunity Insights</a>, a nonprofit think tank based at Harvard University, have studied economic mobility to identify factors that lead to greater outcomes for children and to find potential remedies for obstacles that get in the way.</p><p>“The incomes of Hispanic and Asian Americans are approaching those of white Americans over generations; those of Black Americans and American Indians are not,” the group notes on its website. “Differences in family characteristics — parental marriage rates, education, wealth — and differences in ability explain very little of the black-white intergenerational gap.”</p><p>The group says universal basic income programs, minimum wage increases and other efforts affecting a single generation “can help narrow racial gaps at a given point in time. However, they are less likely to narrow racial disparities in the long run.”</p><p>The group proposes addressing environmental conditions that can be changed. Mentoring programs for Black boys was one example the group listed. But there were several others that caught my eye like: “efforts to reduce racial bias among whites, interventions to reduce discrimination in criminal justice, and efforts to facilitate greater interaction across racial groups.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-160000" name="module-160000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>I wonder how plausible those remedies are in a place like Chicago, the nation’s perennial leader among big cities in racial segregation of Black residents. In every decennial census since 1980, according to <a class="Link" href="https://s4.ad.brown.edu/projects/diversity/Data/data.htm" target="_blank" ><u>data from Brown University</u></a>, Chicago has topped the list among the most populous 25 American cities in segregation measures of Black residents with their Asian, Latino and white counterparts.</p><p>While those figures measure the lack of proximity between Black residents and members of those other groups, I’ve always viewed segregation measurements as a proxy for racial bias — both conscious and unconscious.</p><p>If you’re uncomfortable living among Black people or sending your children to schools with many Black children, you’re probably not likely to trust Black people or hire them. If you’re uncomfortable traveling to Black communities, you’re probably not going to shop there or open a business there. And that lack of social and economic interaction are among the factors driving inequitable economic mobility.</p><p>In addition to being the most segregated big city in America, Chicago may also be the worst big city for Black economic mobility, according to my analysis of data from <a class="Link" href="https://www.opportunityatlas.org/" target="_blank" ><u>The Opportunity Atlas</u></a>, an online tool from Opportunity Insights and the U.S. Census Bureau.</p><p>The tool provides economic mobility figures — essentially the household income at age 35 for individuals born between 1978 and 1983 — by parent income, and child gender, race and ethnicity for more than 70,000 census tracts across the country.</p><p>Among Chicago census tracts, the median household income for low-income Black children at age 35 was just 40%, 50% and 60% of the median household income figures for low-income Asian, white and Latino children at age 35, respectively, according to my analysis. Those percentages were ranked 45th, 50th and 50th, respectively, among similar figures for the nation’s 50 largest cities.</p><p>But bias and discrimination aren’t just racial, they’re also spatial.</p><p>While low-income Black children witnessed lower economic growth than the children of other groups, economic mobility was lower for children of all racial or ethnic groups in census tracts with high percentages of Black residents compared to children in census tracts with low percentages of Black residents.</p><p>The analysis showed a negative correlation among Chicago’s census tracts between the economic mobility figures of low-income Asian, Black, Latino and white children with the share of Black population. Essentially, across the city’s census tracts, as the percentage of Black population increased, the household income at age 35 decreased for low-income children of all racial or ethnic groups, particularly Black and Latino children.</p><p>Conversely, the analysis showed positive correlations for economic mobility of all low-income children with the percentages of Asian and white residents — household income at age 35 increased for low-income children of all groups as the percentages of Asian and white residents increased.</p><p>Most parents want the best for their children. They want their kids to earn more money, own a bigger house, see more of the world and live a better life than they have. Parents will save, sacrifice and even splurge, in some cases, to make their kids’ dreams come true.</p><p>But some things are beyond their control, like how their communities are perceived and how their children are judged. For some, those perceptions work in their favor, and for others it’s just the opposite.</p><p>The data don’t mean that Black children and Black communities can’t achieve economic success — there’s plenty of Black excellence abound to prove that it can be done. But the stereotyping of Black people and Black communities have real consequences, and the persistence of those beliefs only makes an already uphill climb even harder.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/alden-loury" target="_blank" ><i>Alden Loury</i></a><i> is data projects editor for WBEZ and writes a column for the Sun-Times.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-bc0000" name="module-bc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Alden Loury | WBEZ

entries.title: Listen Sunday to WBEZ’s latest ‘Prisoncast!’
entries.summary: <p>What good is education for people in prison? Find out by listening from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. Sunday to “Prisoncast!” on WBEZ-91.5 FM or online at <a class="Link" href="http://wbez.org" target="_blank" ><i><u>wbez.org</u></i></a><i>.</i></p><p>It’s a radio show made with and for people in prisons and jails in Illinois and their loved ones.</p><p>You’ll hear how some incarcerated students say education has been transformative for them — and also why school behind bars is so hard to come by.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2024/12/16/aboutprisoncast" target="_blank" >Prisoncast! FAQ and how you can get involved</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/FlMYxHHAWjk?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="WBEZ's Prisoncast! and Chicago's Mud Theatre Project present "The Story of Violence""></iframe> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Alex Keefe | WBEZ

entries.title: Dear Abby: Eye exams can save more than your vision
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I'm an ophthalmologist, a physician who specializes in medical and surgical eye care, and I'm hoping you can help me debunk a common myth about eye health. Many assume eye disease happens only to older adults. While age does increase the risk of developing certain vision-threatening diseases, such as age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma and cataracts, younger people can also develop vision-threatening eye conditions.</p><p>This is particularly true for the nearly 40 million Americans living with diabetes. Diabetic retinopathy, a complication that damages blood vessels in the retina, is now the leading cause of blindness among working-age adults in the USA. The solution is surprisingly simple — a medical eye exam — but most people aren't getting one.</p><p>Abby, regular eye exams and timely interventions can reduce the risk of severe vision loss by more than 90%. However, only about 60% of people with diabetes receive the annual dilated eye examinations recommended by the American Academy of Ophthalmology. Ophthalmologists have more tools than ever for diagnosing and treating eye diseases.</p><p>For people worried about the cost, the Academy's EyeCare America program may be able to help. ALL healthy adults should have a comprehensive eye exam by age 40, when early signs of cataract, glaucoma and age-related macular degeneration may begin to emerge. — <i>LAURA FINE, M.D., OPHTHALMOLOGIST AND EYECARE AMERICA VOLUNTEER</i></p><p><b>DEAR DR. FINE:</b> I am pleased to share with my readers that EyeCare America has expanded from a seniors-only program. Readers, EyeCare America is a national public service program, and it offers no-out-of-pocket-cost medical eye exams with volunteer ophthalmologists to qualified individuals aged 18 and over. To find out if you or your loved ones are eligible, visit www.aao.org/eyecare-america/patients.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My son and his wife work full time. They have two kids, ages 11 and 9. Their house is filthy. It smells awful, and it's dirty and very dusty. My breathing is affected every time I go to their house.</p><p>I have suggested a housekeeper, but my son refuses to have one. Although his wife works full time, she is the only one who cleans and does the laundry and shopping. My son and the kids do nothing. They have asked me on several occasions to watch their animals when they go on vacation, and I have. However, it is becoming more and more difficult for me to do that because I can't stand being in their house.</p><p>I'm not sure how to tell them without hurting their feelings that I cannot do this anymore. It really is affecting my breathing. Can you help me with how to tell them their house is filthy or maybe what else to say? — <i>COVERING MY NOSE IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR COVERING:</b> If your breathing is being affected when you visit your son's house, you may be allergic to something in the home. It might be dust or, more likely, animal dander. Discuss this with your doctor so you can be tested. Then tell your son and his wife that because it is affecting your health, you can no longer take care of their animals when they travel.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Possibilities and obstacles for those seeking an education in Illinois jails
entries.summary: <div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-1e0000" name="module-1e0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><i>Prisoncast!</i> from WBEZ Chicago 91.5 FM airs <b>Sunday, April 12, from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m.</b> The statewide special broadcast uses the power of radio to connect people inside prisons and jails with their loved ones on the outside.<br><br>The program explores Illinois’ prison system through questions, requests and ideas from people currently and formerly incarcerated in Illinois. It also includes song requests and voicemails from loved ones. Submit yours at <a class="Link" href="mailto:prisoncast@wbez.org" target="_blank" ><u>prisoncast@wbez.org</u></a> or call <b>312-893-2931</b>.</div> </div><p>In a recent visit to Sheridan Correctional Center, 90 minutes southwest of Chicago, Mayor Brandon Johnson spoke with incarcerated students in an hour-long conversation covering a wide range of topics from public education to affordable housing and even peewee football in the suburbs.</p><p>“Take that same energy and help me build an economy that works for everyone so we don’t need prisons,” Johnson said to the students and families of the Northwestern Prison Education Program. Some were dressed in their “visiting gear” — meticulously cleaned, bright white sneakers and crisp prison blues that some had pressed under the mattress in their cells.</p><p>Darvin Henderson, who’s working toward his degree, said he wasn’t raised in an environment where meeting influential politicians felt possible.</p><p>“Those opportunities never even was available,” he said, “and look: I’m in the prison, people would think I’m at the bottom, and yet I’m rubbing elbows with people at the top.”</p><p>The mayor’s visit was organized by Northwestern University. Henderson is one of less than 2% of Illinois’ more than 30,000 incarcerated people enrolled in college-level programs. Years of research show education leads to better outcomes for people getting out of prison. But with only 12 programs in 10 of the 30 Illinois Department of Corrections facilities, getting into a prison college program is impossible for most.</p><p>Education behind bars — its possibilities and obstacles — is the theme of an upcoming special broadcast of<i> Prisoncast!</i> from WBEZ Chicago, a radio program exploring the prison system through questions and requests from people incarcerated in Illinois and loved ones. In the two-hour program on <b>Sunday, April 12, from 2 pm. to 4 p.m.</b>, listeners will hear from inmates, advocates and loved ones on getting an education behind bars and the barriers to continuing college courses once back in the community.</p><p>Also in the broadcast:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-f6bac690-351f-11f1-a45e-8b6a340a1ec0"><li>Female inmates finding creative ways to preserve their dignity through beauty routines </li><li>Why Illinois prisons are still providing abysmal health care, despite years of court oversight</li><li>Thoughts on the power of song from inmates enrolled in the Northwestern Prison Education Program</li><li>One incarcerated man’s life-changing experience with prison education — and why he says Illinois needs more of it</li></ul><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-780000" name="module-780000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Why we make <i>Prisoncast!</i></h3><br>The United States has the <a class="Link" href="https://www.prisonpolicy.org/profiles/US.html" target="_blank" ><u>highest incarceration rate</u></a> in the world. Nearly half of all Americans have had an immediate family member in prison or jail. Yet prison systems, including Illinois’, are designed to be opaque.<br><br>More than 40% of people locked up there were convicted in Cook County, and the vast majority will one day be released. <i>Prisoncast!</i> utilizes a uniquely uncensorable medium — the statewide public radio network — to get practical news and information to people incarcerated here, and to share their untold stories with the outside world.<br><br>Everything on the show comes directly from audience requests, which provides a point of connection for those behind bars and people on the outside. It also provides transparency into a taxpayer-funded system through reporting and investigations on prison conditions, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/health/2026/03/02/health-care-illinois-prisons-consent-decree" target="_blank" ><u>healthcare</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/public-safety/2026/02/06/no-good-time-for-you-the-recordkeeping-snafu-keeping-some-illinoisans-in-prison-longer-than-they-should-be" target="_blank" ><u>state policies</u></a>.<br><br>We know many people in prison have caused profound hurt. <i>Prisoncast!</i> doesn’t shy away from that. Neither do many of the people we’ve interviewed.<br><br>Our approach in producing the show is to understand the people and systems at play. Through this exploration, the public — and incarcerated listeners — may find solutions and inspiration to contribute to a safer community for all.<br><br><h4>How can I listen? </h4><br>In the Chicago area, you can hear the next show live on <b>WBEZ 91.5 FM on Sunday, April 12, at 2 p.m</b>. The show also airs on every <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/2024/12/16/aboutprisoncast" target="_blank" ><u>Illinois Public Radio station</u></a> in the state. If you have a loved one who’s incarcerated, they can listen on an old-school radio or on their tablet’s FM radio app.<br><br><h4>What if I missed the radio broadcast? </h4><br>All previous <i>Prisoncast! </i>episodes, newsletters, investigations and stories are at <a class="Link" href="http://wbez.org/prisoncast" target="_blank" ><u>wbez.org/prisoncast</u></a>. People in Illinois prisons and jails can access our content for free on the Edovo app on their ICS tablets, though it can take a couple weeks for content to upload there. We’re also launching the new <i>Prisoncast!</i> podcast on April 20.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Chicago Sun-Times and WBEZ staff

entries.title: At Expo Chicago, a crew of collectors bond over Black art
entries.summary: <p>For one group of collectors, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/04/03/expo-chicago-art-fair-obama-center-contemporary-galleries-navy-pier-tickets" target="_blank" ><u>Expo Chicago</u></a> is not just an art fair but a family reunion.</p><p>On Thursday afternoon, they trickled into Navy Pier’s Festival Hall, offering enthusiastic hugs and handshakes. The cohort included everyone from health care professionals to military veterans, who traveled from Washington, D.C., Atlanta, Kansas City, Mo., and other major cities. Before touring the galleries, they gathered in the center of the venue for a champagne toast to “building together.”</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>“You can feel the energy in the space,” said collector Naretha Hopson, 48, of St. Louis. “When we have everyone together, the unity, joy and love for art is invaluable.”</p><p>They are part of the Black Collectors Guild, a social group co-founded by Chicago engineer <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/04/30/novice-art-collector-chris-craft-praises-expo-chicago-2025-navy-pier" target="_blank" ><u>Chris Craft</u></a>. Since its launch in 2020, the collective has grown to include more than 100 members around the world. They communicate regularly in a digital group chat, but relish the rare opportunities to meet in person at events. They say the guild has allowed them to build community and share tips in an industry where information and access are gatekept. And they are intentional about supporting Black artists by purchasing their work and advocating for its inclusion in museums.</p><p>“Being active and supporting artists and getting them out there is really what it’s about,” said co-founder <a class="Link" href="https://kcstudio.org/building-a-more-open-inclusive-diverse-arts-scene-dwight-smith/" target="_blank" ><u>Dwight Smith</u></a>, a 55-year old Army veteran who lives in Kansas City, Mo. “And showing folks that Black people do it, too. We like art. I think it’s really important that you see Black influences.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bb0000" name="image-bb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d81b148/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/291b1b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a38b5f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black Collectors Guild member Jordan Porter-Woodruff chats with her wife, Leia Taylor-Woodruff, and friends at EXPO CHICAGO art fair at Navy Pier, Thursday, April 9, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a38b5f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5a09052/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a38b5f4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcf%2F9d%2F57605ea94b959fabde851cf819a9%2Fblackcollectors-260411-026.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The guild communicates regularly in a digital group chat, but relishes the rare opportunities to meet in person at events.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Craft said the group has made him feel less alone as a Black professional.</p><p>“We’re successful, but we’re successful in our own worlds,” said Craft, of Irving Park, who also celebrated his 47th birthday at the expo.</p><p>“You walk into a room and it’s just you. It’s good to know that everybody else is going through that same experience. It is good to have a safe space where we can all talk about different things but also help each other.”</p><p>That means educating each other about different artists across the globe. Equipped with that information, Craft and his wife, Dione, now own more than 200 works of art. He and other guild members have also helped Black artists in their collection get representation and get their pieces shown in art institutions. It’s an attempt to fill gaps in diversity, as works by Black artists only account for 2.2% of acquisitions and 6.3% of exhibitions at U.S. museums, <a class="Link" href="https://news.artnet.com/art-world/full-data-rundown-burns-halperin-report-2227460" target="_blank" ><u>according to a 2022 study</u></a>.</p><p>Smith said he has loaned pieces from his own collection to the Kemper Museum of Contemporary Art and Albrecht-Kemper Museum of Art in Missouri. And he is especially passionate about helping young artists.</p><p>“I think a lot of these status quo institutions get Black artists once they’ve already made a name for themselves,” Smith said. “They’re not doing the heavy lifting. I support the students early.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-580000" name="image-580000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a780451/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/05fcb08/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26e10a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black Collectors Guild member Dwight Smith chats with fellow members at the PATRON Gallery booth in EXPO CHICAGO art fair at Navy Pier, Thursday, April 9, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26e10a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/87ef66b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/26e10a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F0c%2F89484d7f451c97f84728b61387eb%2Fblackcollectors-260411-018.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Being active and supporting artists and getting them out there is really what it’s about,” said co-founder Dwight Smith, a 55-year old Army veteran.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A featured artist at Expo Chicago, Helina Metaferia attested to the impact of the Black Collectors Guild. Black women members from the group purchased two of her sculptures, a wood and brass staff and brass crown. They also donated the latter to be included in the permanent collection of a prominent museum, which will announce the acquisition later this spring. The pieces are displayed by Superposition Gallery, owned by New York resident Storm Ascher.</p><p>“My work upholds the legacies and histories of women of color, particularly Black women,” said Metaferia, an Ethiopian American artist and New Yorker.</p><p>“To be supported by a Black woman gallerist, Black women collectors and then to make sure that [work] gets to be in the museum feels so empowering,” she said. “It’s beyond what I could have dreamed of on my own.”</p><p>After stopping by Superposition Gallery’s booth, the guild members visited an exhibition by Affinity Gallery, which is based in Lagos, Nigeria. There, they admired pieces made from African wax print fabric and acrylic paintings on lace paper.</p><p>“You want to see people that look like you,” said gallerist Olu Affinity. “It gives you more confidence. It’s also a privilege to be able to support Black collectors. It’s important to help them continue to grow their collection.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-be0000" name="image-be0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/316ebbe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8c%2F66b958684ae38d2b70b673dca1ae%2Fblackcollectors-260411-005.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2066501/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8c%2F66b958684ae38d2b70b673dca1ae%2Fblackcollectors-260411-005.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4e8595c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc8%2F8c%2F66b958684ae38d2b70b673dca1ae%2Fblackcollectors-260411-005.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black Collectors Guild Founder Chris Craft and Helina Metaferia (center) discusses her artwork on view at the Superposition Gallery booth in EXPO CHICAGO art fair at Navy Pier, Thursday, April 9, 2026. class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As a collector and a photographer, being a member of the guild is especially beneficial for Jordan Porter-Woodruff, 35, of Woodlawn.</p><p>“It's nice to know what other collectors are thinking or what they’re interested in,” she said. “It also helps inform where my art fits in, too.”</p><p>Porter-Woodruff said Chris Craft helped build her confidence as an artist. He was the first person to buy one of her portraits at her debut art show at Epiphany Center for the Arts in 2023.</p><p>Porter-Woodruff didn’t attend art school and works as a staff photographer for University of Chicago Medicine. But she is pursuing her passion for fine arts photography.</p><p>“I never thought of myself as someone to really have the right to do that,” she said. “[Craft] really helped my career.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1a0000" name="image-1a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aab0397/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59a5d40/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71b378f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black Collectors Guild Founder Chris Craft (center) leads a champagne toast before the group explores the EXPO CHICAGO art fair at Navy Pier, Thursday, April 9, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71b378f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/adabb4d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71b378f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2Fc5%2F026163464f118cc05aeb6cd489e3%2Fblackcollectors-260411-002.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Craft said the Black Collectors Guild has made him feel less alone as a Black professional.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Craft himself credits other Black Collectors Guild members with his own advancement in the art world. He said the network helped him get a seat on the board of the DuSable Black History Museum and Education Center.</p><p>Beyond influence, the guild is also about stewardship, said Chris Eke, a member who also serves on the DuSable Black History Museum board.</p><p>Too often, Black stories are not told by Black people, Eke added.</p><p>“We have to be custodians of the art,” he said. “You can’t control the culture if you don’t own the culture. These are our stories, and they’re priceless.”<br></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: How we photographed Pilsen's annual reenactment of Jesus' crucifixion on Good Friday
entries.summary: <p>This week’s top photo comes from Visual Journalist <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/candace-chambers" target="_blank" >Candace Dane Chambers</a>.</p><h3><b>What makes this photo important?</b></h3><p>The annual “Via Crucis: Living Way of the Cross” reenactment is now in its 49th year. The Good Friday tradition commemorates Jesus Christ’s crucifixion from the Last Supper until his resurrection, with a cast of devotees embodying the biblical characters who betrayed, brutalized and mourned the man who they believe to be the son of God. The story is broadly known to most, even among non-Christians, but the multi-hour performance presents the events in visceral detail.</p><h3><b>How did you get this photo?</b></h3><p>This photo was captured immediately following the reenactment of Jesus’ crucifixion. For much of the performance, both the cast and audience were in motion, walking a long procession on18th Street in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pilsen" >Pilsen</a> while Jesus is forced to carry the cross that will eventually bring him to his death. The crucifixion was staged in a small park with limited space for the performers and the many members of the press covering the event. Understanding that this was the climax of the reenactment, I limited my movement as I photographed out of respect for the religious reverence of the viewers. I sat in the grass surrounded by a group of standing performers and hoped that as they towered above they would obscure me from view. To my surprise, once they took Jesus’ limp body off the cross, they laid him just to the left of where I was positioned. I crawled forward and tucked my lens between the actors where I was able to capture this expressive shot of the Virgin Mary holding her slain son.</p><h3><b>Technical details:</b></h3><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-9460a7e0-1f16-11f1-ad7a-f35fc19f6d46"><li>Equipment: Canon EOS R5 with an RF 28-70mm f/2L lens</li><li>Focal length: 52mm</li><li>Aperture: f/3.2</li><li>Exposure: 1/3200</li><li>ISO: 160</li></ul><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/03/devotees-commemorate-49th-annual-via-crucis-in-pilsen" >Devotees commemorate 49th annual ‘Via Crucis’ in Pilsen</a> </li> </ul> </div>
