entries.author: Candace Dane Chambers

entries.title: WNBA free agency update: Sky sign Azurá Stevens to three-year deal
entries.summary: <p>Azurá Stevens has agreed to a three-year deal to return to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a>, ESPN reported on Saturday. The team confirmed the signing in a statement.</p><p>"We couldn't pass up the opportunity to bring back Azurá, a pivotal part of our championship team," Sky general manager Jeff Pagliocca said. "Stevens brings a versatile element to our frontcourt as a shooter and shot blocker that we're confident will pair nicely with the players we have under contract."</p><p>The Sky want to run a more positionless offense, and Stevens, a stretch big, should fit that system well.</p><p>She's coming off a career year with the Sparks, averaging a career-high 12.8 points per game and shooting 38.1% from 3-point range. She finished second in Most Improved Player voting.</p><p>Stevens was part of the Sky's 2021 championship team and the 2022 squad that finished atop the regular-season standings.</p><p>“I’m truly excited to be coming home to join the Chicago Sky,” Stevens said in a statement. “This city means everything to me. The culture, the people, the passion for the game. To have the opportunity to come back to Chicago is amazing. I’m ready to get to work, embrace the moment, and give everything I have. Let’s work.”</p><p>Stevens will be a key piece of the Sky's roster overhaul. To kick off free agency, the Sky traded forward Angel Reese — the league's leading rebounder — to the Dream for future draft picks.</p><p>Stevens now joins Kamilla Cardoso, Hailey Van Lith and Maddy Westbeld as the only players under contract. Courtney Vandersloot also announced her intention to return last season, and earlier this week, the Sky extended a qualifying offer to Sevgi Uzun.</p><p>The Sky are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/09/sky-reportedly-in-talks-to-trade-ariel-atkins-for-rickea-jackson" >reportedly in talks with the Sparks about trading Ariel Atkins for Rickea Jackson</a>. The Sparks' frontcourt got crowded when the team added Nneka Ogwumike in free agency; they also have Cameron Brink under contract, and the team is expected to re-sign Dearica Hamby.</p><p>The Sky have the No. 5 pick in the upcoming draft, which could result in a player like Flau’jae Johnson (LSU) or Kiki Rice (UCLA). They could also flip that asset in a trade.</p><p>The college draft will take place on April 13 and training camp opens on April 19.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Elderly man found dead on CTA train in the Loop
entries.summary: <p>Chicago police are investigating after an elderly man was found dead on a CTA train early Saturday in the Loop. </p><p>The man, whose exact age was not known, was seated on a Blue Line train when passengers realized he was unresponsive around 1 a.m. in the 300 block of South Dearborn Street, Chicago police said. </p><p>The man was pronounced dead at the scene, police said. His name hasn't been released. </p><p>The cause of death was unknown pending an autopsy from the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Sky land Skylar Diggins, make biggest free-agency splash in years
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> pulled it off.</p><p>Six-time All-WNBA guard Skylar Diggins will sign with the Sky, the team announced Saturday. ESPN reported that it's a two-year deal.</p><p>"Skylar is a player we have coveted for years," general manager Jeff Pagliocca said. "We are ecstatic to bring her to Chicago. She is one of the all-time elite passers in our game and continues to play at a high level on both sides of the ball."</p><p>Pagliocca took a swing and a miss on Diggins in the 2024 offseason. Instead she signed with the Storm, where she averaged 15.3 points and 6.2 assists over the last two years.</p><p>Landing her now changes the team's trajectory.</p><p>The Sky now have Diggins, Kamilla Cardoso, Azura Stevens, Jacy Sheldon, Hailey Van Lith and Maddy Westbeld under contract.</p><p>They're also reportedly in talks to trade Ariel Atkins to the Sparks for Rickea Jackson.</p><p>A starting lineup of Diggins, Cardoso, Stevens and Jackson may sound a little odd, and would still need a shooting guard. But it has talent, size and dynamism — the makings of a team that could compete for a playoff spot.</p><p>The Sky looked to be stumbling out of the gates in free agency when they dealt away Angel Reese — widely considered the franchise's cornerstone — to the Dream for future picks.</p><p>But Diggins' arrival signals a turnaround. She could be the Sky's most impactful free-agent addition since Emma Meesseman in 2022.</p><p>"Diggins is the kind of player who elevates everyone around her," coach Tyler Marsh said. "Skylar is a true veteran leader, and she possesses unmatched speed and shot creation potential. I can't wait to work with her."</p><p>Diggins, 35, grew up 90 miles southeast of Chicago in South Bend, Indiana.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Sky trade for Jacy Sheldon, give up 2028 first-round pick
entries.summary: <p>Have the Sky finally found their point guard of the future?</p><p>The team announced the acquisition of Jacy Sheldon from Washington, adding a 25-year-old guard known for relentless on-ball defense and improved shooting.</p><p>"Jacy made an impression on us with her toughness, competitiveness and ability to compete on both ends of the court," general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. "She has proven to be a great young player who can shoot the ball and defend in this league. Jacy fits what we are looking for seamlessly."</p><p>Sheldon, the No. 5 pick in the 2024 draft, bounced around early in her career — traded to Connecticut after her rookie season in Dallas, then dealt to Washington midway through 2025. But her stock rose last season as she bumped her 3-point percentage from 30.9% to 39.6%.</p><p>To land her, Chicago gave up its 2028 first-round pick. The Mystics also holds the right to swap the Sky's 2027 first-rounder, acquired in the Ariel Atkins deal last year.</p><p>The Sky's long term plans for the point-guard spot have been up in the air. Courtney Vandersloot is expected back from ACL injury but will likely miss time early, and the front office acknowledges she won't be able to play 35 minutes a game. </p><p>The Sky also drafted combo guard Hailey Van Lith at No. 11 in 2024, but she struggled to adapt to the pros. Earlier this week, the Sky extended a qualifying offer to Sevgi Uzun, a 28-year old point guard who joined the team on a rest-of-season contract in 2025.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Start of White Sox game at Royals delayed by rain
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — The start of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> game against the Royals was delayed Saturday afternoon due to rain in Kansas City. </p><p>Crews pulled the tarp over the infield at Kauffman Stadium about 20 minutes before the scheduled 3:10 p.m. first pitch, which was pushed back to 3:35 p.m. after a quick shower rolled through the area. </p><p>Sox starter Erick Fedde (0-2, 4.09 ERA) was scheduled to take on Michael Wacha (1-0, 0.69).</p><p>The Sox won the first game of the four-game set Thursday 2-0, but lost by the same score Friday. </p><p>Rain was also forecast for Sunday's series finale, scheduled for 1:10 p.m.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Bulls forward Matas Buzelis likely done for the year but talks future
entries.summary: <p>Matas Buzelis was at a loss for words.</p><p>“I don’t really know … what did I accomplish this year?” the Bulls forward replied when asked by the media about his 2025-26 campaign.</p><p>When told he was one of six players in NBA history to have at least 150 made three-pointers and 100 blocks, yeah, he was taken back. Especially when names like Dirk Nowitzki and Kevin Durant were in that company.</p><p>It’s amazing how the mood quickly changed for the 21-year-old when given new information.</p><p>“That’s crazy, yeah,” Buzelis said Friday night after the loss to Orlando. “It definitely means a lot to me for sure. It shows the work that I put in during the summer, during the season, the dedication, countless hours in the gym, and there’s just more. I want to accomplish more, and more for our team.”</p><p>He’ll likely have to wait on both fronts as a badly sprained ankle suffered against the Magic in the fourth has likely put his final start in Dallas on life support.</p><p>It’s still a season Buzelis should be proud of from an individual standpoint. He doubled his scoring average from his rookie season, going from 8.6 points per game to 16.3, he was stronger on the defensive end from a physicality standpoint with a lot of room to improve, and he became a legit top 10 rim protector in the league.</p><p>Is he a definitive high-ceiling All-Star yet? No, but he’s got the highest ceiling on this Bulls roster and it’s not even close.</p><p>And maybe more importantly, he’s got a work ethic.</p><p>“I know this summer I am going to work my butt off,” Buzelis insisted. “The whole summer, it’s going to be a hard summer for me. My body getting stronger and sharpening pretty much everything, but mainly the body part, the shot, the handle, just everything. The main thing will be (adding) the strength for me.</p><p>“I always want more for myself and the team. I definitely believe that I am (a foundation piece). I know for a fact I am only going to get better. If they want me here and they believe in me, I’m here. I believe I am definitely a big piece, and I am going to continue to work and put my head down and grind to show it.”</p><p>All the things the Bulls should be excited to hear. If only there were key figures in place to hear it.</p><p>Executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley were both fired on Monday, and the future of Billy Donovan is still up in the air with the coach expected to meet with ownership next week and discuss the plans moving forward and if Donovan wants to even be a part of those plans.</p><p>Buzelis discussed both of those topics in what was likely his exit interview with the media.</p><p>“It was tough,” Buzelis said of the firings. “The organization is going to do what they feel is best, but it’s unfortunate because those guys drafted me, believed in me. They were like family to me. Those guys are going to be good. They’re great people. They work really hard.”</p><p>As for Donovan, Buzelis continued his high praise of the coach and the tough love in which Donovan coaches him.</p><p>“I want him to stay,” Buzelis said. “I’m riding with him forever.”</p><p>“I remember sitting down with Billy over the summer and talking about how I don’t know how to win. It’s a team sport and hard to win basketball games, but you can be a winning player out there and do what it takes for the team to win. I haven’t really been on a team where I have to pave the way with my teammates to lead them to victory in games. I told them I don’t know what to do and they taught me. I want to show them this season I am a winning player and I have those characteristics.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: 'Enough of the idolatry of self and money!' Pope Leo blasts 'delusion of omnipotence' fueling war in Iran
entries.summary: <p>ROME — In his strongest words yet, Pope Leo XIV on Saturday denounced the “delusion of omnipotence” that is fueling the U.S.-Israel war in Iran and demanded political leaders stop and negotiate peace.</p><p>Leo presided over an evening prayer service in St. Peter’s Basilica on the same day the United States and Iran began face-to-face negotiations in Pakistan as a fragile ceasefire held.</p><p>History’s first U.S.-born pope didn’t mention the United States or President Donald Trump in his prayer, which was planned before the talks were announced. But Leo’s tone and message appeared directed at Trump and U.S. officials, who have boasted of U.S. military superiority and justified the war in religious terms.</p><p>“Enough of the idolatry of self and money!” Leo said. “Enough of the display of power! Enough of war!”</p><p>In the basilica pews was the archbishop of Tehran, Belgian Cardinal Dominique Joseph Mathieu. The U.S. was represented in the diplomatic corps by its deputy chief of mission, Laura Hochla, the U.S. Embassy said.</p><p>In the first weeks of the war, the pope, who was born in Chicago and raised in Dolton, was initially reluctant to publicly condemn the violence and limited his comments to muted appeals for peace and dialogue. But Leo stepped up his criticism starting on Palm Sunday. And this week, he said Trump’s threat to annihilate Iranian civilization was “truly unacceptable” and called for dialogue to prevail.</p><p>On Saturday, Leo called for all people of good will to pray for peace and demand an end to war from their political leaders. The evening vigil in Rome, which featured Scripture readings and meditative recitation of the rosary prayers, was taking place as simultaneous local prayer services were being held in the U.S. and beyond.</p><p>Praying for peace, Leo said, was a way to “break the demonic cycle of evil” in order to build instead the kingdom of God where there are no swords, drones or “unjust profit.”</p><p>“It is here that we find a bulwark against that delusion of omnipotence that surrounds us and is becoming increasingly unpredictable and aggressive,” he said. “Even the holy Name of God, the God of life, is being dragged into discourses of death.”</p><p>Leaders have used religion to justify their actions in the war. U.S. officials and especially Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth have invoked their Christian faith to cast the U.S. as a Christian nation trying to vanquish its foes.</p><p>Leo has said God doesn’t bless any war, and certainly not those who drop bombs.</p><p>Leo presided over the service sitting off to the side of the altar on a white throne, wearing his formal red cape and liturgical stole and praying with a rosary in his hands. Many of the priests and nuns in the pews fingered rosary beads as the “Our Father” and “Hail Mary” prayers were recited.</p><p>The Vatican is particularly concerned about the spillover of Israel’s war against Hezbollah in Lebanon, given the plight of Christian communities in the south.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Review: Florence and the Machine casts a spell in cathartic performance
entries.summary: <p>It’s wisely been said that if you give a woman pain, she’ll turn it into power. For Florence Welch, she’s also turned it into one of the most cathartic albums and concerts of the year.</p><p>It was evident as the British songbird returned to the Chicago area Friday night to unfurl the layered compositions on the 2025 opus, “Everybody Scream,” which explores the extent of feminine strength and human determination.</p><p>Released on Halloween, the record details Welch’s own personal tragedy and the journey to find her way back from the abyss after suffering a devastating miscarriage and ectopic pregnancy in 2023 that necessitated emergency surgery to save her life. The impact was so profound that it netted a full album as well as tour partnerships with Planned Parenthood and Doctors Without Borders to provide critical medical care to others around the world.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-770000" name="image-770000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ec89a0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7dc29f8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88cc5eb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Florence and the Machine performs in Minneapolis. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88cc5eb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/407fe64/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88cc5eb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2Fae%2F78e423214ae191ea15d358ac3356%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0015hi.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Florence and the Machine performs in Minneapolis. The British band brought its Everybody Scream tour to Allstate Arena Friday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Lillie Eiger</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>With this magnitude, what unfolded at the Allstate Arena Friday wasn’t exactly a concert, but more of a ritual where collective trauma and suffering were exorcised in a type of release that normally would’ve cost a lot more money in therapy than the price of admission.</p><p>“Whatever you’ve been through or whatever you’re going through, know that I’m with you,” Welch said at the top of the show to set the tone for the passionate crowd, many of whom came dressed in Welch-approved flower crowns, boho skirts and shawls and clung to her every word.</p><p>If it all sounds witchy and mystical and new agey, it was — and it was beautiful. For two hours, Welch summoned something deep inside the human psyche as she was joined in sound and fury by her incredible backing band, the Machine, that added violins and harp to the baroque score in addition to saxophone, drums and keys. Not to mention a devoted coven of Lizzie Borden-looking backing dancers/actors who performed like they were possessed as they contorted their bodies and succumbed to Welch’s whims when she beckoned them.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-aa0000" name="image-aa0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d60067c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f7be2ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a45f16/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Florence Welch of Florence and the Machine performs in Minneapolis. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a45f16/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b9556cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0a45f16/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5464x3067+0+2563/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F31%2F714c74334cf5ae3c6ef884fed433%2Ffatm-estour-us-17-02-26-minneapolis-le-c-0285hi.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Florence Welch of Florence and the Machine performs in Minneapolis. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Lillie Eiger</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For years, Welch has been hailed as a descendant of Stevie Nicks and Kate Bush for her witchy romanticism, and she has embraced that persona in her new era, where she plays with the concept of witchcraft as a means to unlock personal power and connect to Mother Earth for answers. For Welch, studying this connection over the past few years became a lifesaver. At its core, “Everybody Scream” challenges the idea that women are too “emotional” and proffers that we’d all feel a lot better if we just let it out once in a while. Many did so on Welch’s cue during the opening song, and it produced an eerie calm that blanketed the arena the rest of the night.</p><p>“Everybody Scream” is not an easy album to unpack and Welch did so delicately throughout the night as she sang about gods and devils, moons and stars and the cosmic connection of creating life and losing it. The most heartbreaking moment came on “You Can Have It All” — recently added to the North American set list as Welch gets more comfortable singing lyrics about the “vision of my daughter” and wonders, “Am I a woman now?” after her experience.</p><p>“This next song is a very hard song to sing, but it feels very important to sing it here,” she said to preface the performance, which featured her strongest vocal delivery of the night.</p><p>“Sympathy Magic” was another turning point as Welch used the moment to immerse herself in the crowd, delivering the song’s lyrics — “come on, I can take it, give me everything you got, what else, what else, what else” — directly to people in the front row who were visibly emotional as they took her message to heart.</p><p>To emphasize that no one is in the dark alone, Welch bade everyone to join together in letting it all out in a physically moving performance of “Dog Days Are Over” before purposely ending the set with the new song “And Love,” where she assures “peace is coming.”</p><p>“When I was writing this album, one of my darker records, I wanted to write a song that, if songs are spells or prayers or prophecies, let this be the one that comes true,” she shared in her final summon. “Let this be the one that is realized, not just for me but for all of you here and for the whole world. I know that seems impossible, but maybe if we can sing it together, we can believe it to be true.”</p><p><b></b></p><h3>Set list for Florence and the Machine April 10, 2026 show at Allstate Arena</h3><p>Everybody Scream<br>Witch Dance<br>Shake It Out<br>Seven Devils<br>Big God<br>Daffodil<br>Which Witch<br>Cosmic Love<br>Spectrum<br>You Can Have It All<br>Music by Men<br>Buckle<br>King<br>The Old Religion<br>Howl<br>Heaven Is Here<br>Sympathy Magic</p><p><b>Encore:</b><br>One of the Greats<br>Dog Days Are Over<br>Free<br>And Love</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: 2 teen boys wounded in Brighton Park shooting
entries.summary: <p>Two teenage boys were shot and wounded Saturday afternoon in the Brighton Park neighborhood.</p><p>The boys, ages 14 and 15, were near a sidewalk about 5:50 p.m. in the 3800 block of South Artesian Avenue when someone opened fire, Chicago police said.</p><p>The younger boy suffered gunshot wounds to the stomach and leg. He was taken to Stroger Hospital, where he was listed in serious condition, police said.</p><p>The other boy was shot in the arm and was taken to the same hospital in good condition, police said.</p><p>No arrests were reported.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: White Sox bats silent again in second straight shutout loss to Royals
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> hitters were seeing double Saturday afternoon as they were shut out by the Royals 2-0 for a second straight game, marking 20 scoreless innings from a lifeless offense that has gone a week without a home run. </p><p>The hitting drought wasted <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/11/davis-martin-white-sox-strong-start" >another solid performance</a> from starter <b>Erick Fedde</b>, who gave up a solo home run to <b>Maikel Garcia</b> on his first pitch but only scattered two other hits over five innings with four strikeouts and a walk.</p><p>The Sox mustered four hits — including doubles from <b>Andrew Benintendi</b> and <b>Lenyn Sosa</b>, as well as rookie third baseman <b>Tanner Murray</b>’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/04/05/white-sox-rookie-tanner-murray-makes-mlb-debut-as-everson-pereira-goes-on-il" target="_blank" ><u>first career</u></a> base hit — but couldn’t do anything with them as Royals starter <b>Michael Wacha</b> breezed through eight innings with seven strikeouts. </p><p>KC got insurance in the eighth with a double from Garcia, who later scored on a sacrifice fly.</p><p>After some <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/30/white-sox-vargas-grand-slam-first-win-season" target="_blank" ><u>first-week fireworks</u></a>, the Sox have scored just eight runs in their past six games, while their hitters haven’t gone yard since <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> hit <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/04/munetaka-murakami-home-run-powers-white-sox-past-blue-jays" >his first Chicago homer</a> against the Blue Jays April 4. Sox hitters have fanned 60 times since then.</p><p>"A lot of times when you are not getting hits, it feels like you have no energy. That hasn’t been the case for us," manager <b>Will Venable</b> said. "I think the guys are in a good frame of mind. We just have to turn some fastballs around, put some balls in play."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Duncan Davitt throws during the eighth inning Friday at Kansas City.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Charlie Riedel/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Cannon gets call after Davitt’s first draft</b></h3><p>Sportswriter-turned-MLB pitcher <b>Duncan Davitt</b>, who made his big-league debut in the eighth inning of Friday's loss, was sent back to Triple-A <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/18/jonathan-cannon-feeling-more-confident-in-2026-with-new-sinker-grip" >in exchange</a> for <b>Jonathan Cannon</b>, who was expected to pitch in a bulk role Sunday <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/06/grant-taylor-opener-white-sox" >behind opener</a> <b>Grant Taylor</b>. Outfielder <b>Everson Pereira</b> was en route to Charlotte as well for a rehab stint in his recovery from a sprained ankle. </p><p>Davitt, who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/09/duncan-davitt-white-sox-rookie-sports-writer-indianola" target="_blank" ><u>moonlights as a high school sports reporter</u></a> for an Iowa newspaper owned by his parents, walked the first batter he faced, then induced a double play ball from the All-Star third baseman<b> </b>Garcia and a flyout from superstar<b> Bobby Witt Jr.</b></p><p>"There's something to be said about being forged in fire, so if you go through those two guys and manage to come out unscathed, it's a nice learning experience and an, 'OK, I can do this,' kind of a moment,” Davitt said.</p><p>He grew up a Yankees fan but regularly made the three-hour trip from the Des Moines area for games at Kauffman Stadium, where he had plenty of family and friends in attendance for his first outing.</p><p>"I'm not going to lie, I tried to keep my eyes from going up into the stands, just because it's a lot bigger venue than most of the places down in the minor leagues. But the eyes went up anyway,” Davitt said. “Sitting in the dugout afterward, and I was going to meet some family… seeing the fireworks, it's a feeling unmatched. You can't get anything better."</p><p>He wrote a column for the Indianola Independent Advocate that was published online after his Thursday call-up, but it’ll be a while before he’s back in print.</p><p>“I think ‘missed deadline’ is a good way to put it,” Davitt said. “I'm sure there's something coming down eventually."</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Black doulas work to bridge health disparities for Black mothers
entries.summary: <p>Zetanefert Zipewtu says she didn’t choose to become a doula — the work chose her.</p><p>She became certified after supporting a close friend through pregnancy. Now, almost 15 years later, Zipewtu says she sees the vital role doulas play in supporting expectant mothers across the Black and Brown community.</p><p>“A doula is a gap filler,” Zipewtu said on Saturday at a community baby shower at the Garfield Park Fieldhouse on Chicago's West Side to celebrate Black maternal health. “Our job is to ensure that expecting people have the information, emotional support and physical care they need to achieve their birth and postpartum goals.”</p><p>Zipewtu, a doula at Our Mothers’ Light Birth and Baby Village, said she prioritizes caring for Black and Brown mothers. Black women are more than twice as likely as white women to die from pregnancy-related causes, according to the <a class="Link" href="https://dph.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/idph/publications/idph/topics-and-services/life-stages-populations/maternal-child-family-health-services/maternal-health/maternal-mortality-data-report-2025.pdf" target="_blank" >2021-2 data from the Illinois Department of Public Health</a>. Black Illinoisans have the highest maternal mortality rate in the state, at 78 deaths per 100,000 live births.</p><p>Zipewtu said doulas aren’t the solution to mortality rate, but can contribute to making a change. “We can't have a shift in Black maternal health if [expecting mothers] don't have people who understand them and identify with them,” she said.</p><p>Saturday marked Global Black Doula Day and the start of Black Maternal Health Week, a campaign launched by Black Mamas Matter Alliance to raise awareness, drive activism and build community around the experiences of Black mothers, according to Ayesha Jaco, executive director of the nonprofit West Side United.</p><p>The event, hosted by West Side United and Sankofa Wellness Village, aimed to connect expectant and postpartum mothers on the city’s West Side with community resources — a population that often distrusts the health care system.</p><p>“What I've seen is that the lack of trust isn't misplaced,” Zipewtu said as she shared information about doula services with expectant mothers at the event.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-940000" name="image-940000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6657bdb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2F0c%2F5a26f9674b66967c79a3d372cc50%2Fblackdouladay-260412-04.jpg alt="A portrait of pregnant mother Aleesya Burton, who attended the Black Maternal Health Week baby shower at the Garfield Park Fieldhouse on Saturday." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a4c104d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2F0c%2F5a26f9674b66967c79a3d372cc50%2Fblackdouladay-260412-04.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/431f016/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2F0c%2F5a26f9674b66967c79a3d372cc50%2Fblackdouladay-260412-04.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a4c104d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2F0c%2F5a26f9674b66967c79a3d372cc50%2Fblackdouladay-260412-04.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Expecting mother Aleesya Burton attended the Black Maternal Health Week baby shower at the Garfield Park Fieldhouse on Saturday. She says support from a doula and a midwife in the past have made a difference in her birthing experiences.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/For Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For that reason, Aleesya Burton of Auburn Gresham on the South Side, chose to give birth to all three of her children at home with the help of a midwife. She plans to do the same with her fourth.</p><p>“I feel like the best environment is my home,” said Burton, who is 26 weeks pregnant.</p><p>Burton, 31, has had the help of a doula in past pregnancies and said that made a significant difference.</p><p>“Whether they give birth in the hospital or at home, it’s so important to have someone that supports you and understands the culture you’re coming from,” said Burton.</p><p>Zipewtu said she’s seen the mistreatment of Black mothers giving birth.</p><p>“Women are at their most vulnerable,” said Zipewtu. “And even if their family is there, the family is also very vulnerable at that moment.”</p><p>Jaco said she’s met expectant mothers who have foregone health care because of fear. The organization is working to build trust to help improve their access to health care. </p><p>“There’s hesitancy around going in to see a provider because they may not understand you based on your background,” said Jaco. “We really hope that we're breaking down some of those silos.”</p><p>Despite the challenges, Zipewtu says her doula work is worth it when she sees mothers holding their children and getting the best birthing experience possible.</p><p>“There are so many doulas out there who want to help,” said Zipewtu.</p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: WNBA free agency update: Sky sign Rachel Banham
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> are rounding out their depth.</p><p>After signing backup center Elizabeth Williams on Friday, the team announced they’re bringing back shooting guard Rachel Banham.</p><p>“[Banham] had one of the all-time great shooting seasons for the Sky last year,” general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. “We’re ready to get back to work with a competitive, high-character individual.”</p><p>Last season Banham shot 35.7% from the 3-point line and averaged a career-high 9 points per game.</p>
entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Big contract before making an impact? The Cubs' Scott Kingery has been there
entries.summary: <p>They would seem worlds apart. Cubs infielder Scott Kingery, No. 12 on your scorecard and 32 years old at the end of the month, has played just 74 games in the big leagues this decade, just three pinch-running appearances so far as a Cub. His big-league existence is day to day. He has spent almost all of the last three seasons in the minors.</p><p></p><p>In the other dugout, Pirates shortstop Konnor Griffin, the anointed one, 20 years old in a dozen days, a shortstop of considerable size (6-foot-3, 222 pounds), limitless potential, and almost unimaginable financial security for a player of his experience. Five days into his major-league career, Griffin signed a nine-year, $140 million contract, so confident are the Pirates that he is destined for stardom.</p><p></p><p>“He’s super talented,’’ said Jed Hoyer, the Cubs’ president of baseball operations. “Obviously, he was a guy we talked about in the draft, and he's had as fast a development path as I can ever remember. He's an exciting young star in the game, and it'll be fun to watch him in person.’’</p><p></p><p>Hoyer is not the only member of his family enchanted by Griffin. He said his kids went on the Pirates’ website, looking for gear.</p><p></p><p>“I think [the Pirates] just dropped their City Connect yesterday, and my kids were looking at it and his stuff, and it was sold out in almost every imaginable way,’’ Hoyer said. “It’s great for baseball to have young players like this, that people are so excited about.’’</p><p></p><p>You can look far and wide in the Wrigleyville souvenir shops, and you won’t find a Scott Kingery Cubs jersey. But it wasn’t so long ago, when Kingery was known as Scotty Jetpax, he was being favorably compared to former MVP and four-time All-Star Dustin Pedroia and was the toast of Philadelphia. Scott Kingery was judged a star in the making, even before he had played a single day in the big leagues.</p><p></p><p>At the end of spring training in 2018, the Phillies signed Kingery to a six-year, $24 million deal. He was 23 years old when he made a successful big-league debut, with two hits and a stolen base. He was on his way…until he wasn’t. His rookie season was a struggle, compounded by playing an unfamiliar position [shortstop instead of second base]. He rebounded in 2019 with his best season in the big leagues, posting a .788 OPS with 19 home runs, but was wiped out in the pandemic in 2020, contracting a virulent case of coronavirus.</p><p></p><p>When he struggled in spring training in 2021, he was demoted to Triple-A and the road back proved a hard one, complicated by ineffectiveness and a string of health issues, including season-ending shoulder surgery in 2021 that caused him to miss the start of the following season. In 2024, Scotty Jetpax was traded to the Angels, where he played just 19 games.</p><p></p><p>The Cubs signed him as a minor-league free agent last December.</p><p></p><p>In a quiet Cubs clubhouse the other morning, Kingery reflected on his experience of not meeting exalted expectations, the security that came with the contract and the pressures written into the fine print.</p><p></p><p>“I think at first people are going to be excited to get a look at the person who just signed the contract,’’ Kingery said. “If you go out on the field and ensure that you can play a little bit, I don’t think they think about the contract. For me, I think that came a little later.’’</p><p></p><p>The contract became a cudgel when Kingery struggled.</p><p></p><p>“I think a lot of people would look at it and say, you just signed a contract, you have guaranteed money,’’ he said. “No need to worry, just go and play baseball. For me, since I had never played in the big leagues, I think there was a little more pressure on myself to prove that I was worth what they just paid me, to live up to the contract and play up to the contract.</p><p></p><p>“But looking back on it, when I was on the field I wasn’t thinking about that. I was super excited, but at the same time it was like, ‘OK, they pay me for a reason, I want to go out in the field and show why.’’</p><p></p><p>For Connor Griffin, just as there once was for Scott Kingery, the line between dreams fulfilled and hopes dashed is a narrow one.</p><p></p><p>Cubs veteran second baseman Nico Hoerner, for one, believes the risk is worth it for the Pirates.</p><p></p><p>“We’ve seen players in the past that debut at a young age and go on to have amazing careers,’’ Hoerner said. “Obviously the organization has a ton of confidence in him, and his skill set is so impressive across all parts of the game, from defense to base running and power. He seems like an easy player to believe in overall.’’<br></p>
entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: White Sox workhorse Davis Martin pacing steady rotation after tough start to season
entries.summary: <p>KANSAS CITY, Mo. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a> right-hander Davis Martin has been doing this for a while, but not quite with this level of assurance.</p><p>Martin, 29 and the longest-tenured Sox pitcher, turned in his strongest start in his third outing of the season Friday against the Royals, striking out three with no walks in seven innings in a tough-luck 2-0 loss.</p><p>Martin’s strong introduction to his second full season has buoyed a rotation that sank fast in the first week, only to rebound through its second and third turns.</p><p>And for a veteran leader on the staff, it all comes down to knowing himself.</p><p>‘‘This is probably the best knowledge of my stuff,’’ said Martin, who made his debut with the Sox in 2022 before having Tommy John surgery. ‘‘Obviously, there’s still so much more that I feel like I can accomplish and so much more refining that I think I can do. But from this year to last year, I feel like I know what pitches are working, I know how to use them and then the second and third time through the lineup [how] I probably want to adjust and go from there.’’</p><p>It was the second consecutive quality start from Martin, who’s 2-1 with a 2.50 ERA, 15 strikeouts and four walks in 18 innings. There’s not much flashy about his 94 mph four-seamer, but a kick changeup he refined last season and a sinker opponents have hit only .091 against so far have kept hitters on edge.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8b0000" name="image-8b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="Reds White Sox Spring Baseball" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/917e752/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4398x2468+0+232/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2F2e%2Fcccd0f2e4df4943304d6c498a2ba%2Freds-white-sox-spring-baseball.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cdab1ef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4398x2468+0+232/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2F2e%2Fcccd0f2e4df4943304d6c498a2ba%2Freds-white-sox-spring-baseball.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/917e752/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4398x2468+0+232/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7c%2F2e%2Fcccd0f2e4df4943304d6c498a2ba%2Freds-white-sox-spring-baseball.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Davis Martin delivers in the second inning during spring training baseball game against the Cincinnati Reds, Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026, in Phoenix.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brynn Anderson/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Consistency with those pitches and a quick pace to boot might recall some other all-time Sox fan favorites. Martin made quick work of the Royals on a night in which counterpart Kris Bubic struck out 11 Sox in a clean one hour and 55 minutes. It was the fastest Sox game since July 6, 2015, when Chris Sale outdueled Mark Buehrle — then with the Blue Jays — in a cool one hour and 54 minutes.</p><p>‘‘Davis, that’s as good as we’ve seen him pounding the zone,’’ manager Will Venable said of a pitcher who often has flown under the radar. ‘‘I thought he had command of all his pitches. It was awesome.</p><p></p><p>‘‘He works so hard and puts himself in such a good position to continue to progress and do well. Every outing . . . it’s better and better. He has good stuff, throws strikes and he’s not scared. Good ingredients to have a good starting pitcher.’’</p><p></p><p>For his part, Martin still sees plenty of room for improvement.</p><p></p><p>‘‘It feels good,’’ he said. ‘‘It’s still so early. There’s a lot of good stuff that we can take from this, but there’s also a lot of things that I want to do better. I want to have better kill counts; I want to execute 0-2 better. Don’t have to do too much more. And don’t try to reinvent the wheel.’’</p><p></p><p>Offense has been hard to come by across the majors in early April, but Sox pitchers have rebounded from an atrocious first six games in which the staff stumbled to an 8.63 ERA. Entering play Saturday, the 2.25 ERA they had posted since April 3 was the second-best in MLB, with the starters’ collective mark of 1.19 leading the majors.</p><p></p><p>While Opening Day starter <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/04/08/white-sox-option-opening-day-starter-shane-smith-to-triple-a-charlotte" target="_blank" >Shane Smith</a> figures things out down at Triple-A Charlotte, left-hander <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/10/anthony-kay-jac-caglianone-white-sox-royals-hit-by-pitch" target="_blank" >Anthony Kay</a> has posted a 2.45 ERA in his three outings, right-hander Erick Fedde is at 3.38 and right-hander Sean Burke has settled down to 3.60.</p><p></p><p>A key component of that positive trajectory is a tight-knit group getting closer for the seasonlong marathon, Martin said.</p><p></p><p>‘‘To be together for 10 [or] 12 hours a day, seven days a week, that camaraderie starts building at a quick pace,’’ he said. ‘‘I come in, and Anthony’s already busting my chops about how I gave up a home run. There’s a competitive, fun friendship thing there, and we’re doing a good job. Gotta keep doing it.’’</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Back from the brink: The Sky's offseason is starting to make sense
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> are turning this free agency period around.</p><p>They stumbled out of the gates when they dealt away Angel Reese — a franchise pillar — to the Dream for future picks. But their moves since then have brightened the outlook. Best case, they'll be fighting for a playoff spot. At worst, they'll be a fun team to watch.</p><p>The key signing was six-time All-WNBA guard Skylar Diggins. She's averaged 15.3 points and 6.2 assists with Seattle over the last two seasons.</p><p>"Skylar is a player we have coveted for years," general manager Jeff Pagliocca said in a statement. "We are ecstatic to bring her to Chicago. She is one of the all-time elite passers in our game and continues to play at a high level on both sides of the ball."</p><p>Points for persistence: Pagliocca swung and missed on Diggins in 2024, his first season as GM. Now Diggins might be the franchise's best free-agency signing since Emma Meesseman in 2022.</p><p>"Diggins is the kind of player who elevates everyone around her," coach Tyler Marsh said in a statement. "[She] is a true veteran leader, and she possesses unmatched speed and shot creation potential. I can't wait to work with her."</p><p>On top of adding star power, the Sky's other offseason priority was improving their spacing. The Reese and Kamilla Cardoso frontcourt pairing never fully quite clicked, since neither shoots much from the outside. That hampered the "pace-and-space" system that Marsh wants to run. </p><p>Enter Azurá Stevens.</p><p>The 6-6 stretch big is coming off a career year with the Sparks, averaging a career-high 12.8 points and shooting 38.1 percent from 3-point range. She finished second in Most Improved Player voting.</p><p>"I'm truly excited to be coming home to join the Chicago Sky," Stevens said in a statement. "This city means everything to me. The culture, the people, the passion for the game. To have the opportunity to come back to Chicago is amazing. I'm ready to get to work, embrace the moment, and give everything I have. Let's work."</p><p>Stevens was part of the Sky's 2021 championship team and the 2022 squad that finished atop the regular-season standings. Last season, playing for the Sparks, she torched the Sky for 21 points on 8-of-11 shooting. </p><p>Marsh's halftime message that day: "Don't let Azurá shoot anymore."</p><p>Marsh will be relieved to have her on his side this season. And he may have more firepower coming.</p><p>The Sky are reportedly in talks to trade Ariel Atkins to the Sparks for Rickea Jackson, another dynamic scorer from the 2024 draft class. </p><p>If the deal goes through, the Sky could be looking at a starting lineup of Diggins, Cardoso, Stevens and Jackson. That's plenty of talent, size and a lot more shot creation than last year's group.</p><p>It looks like a playoff-caliber group on paper. </p><p>But their ceiling still depends on how they fill out their bench. The Sky resigned reserves <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/04/10/wnba-free-agency-update-sky-sign-elizabeth-williams-to-two-year-deal" target="_blank" >Elizabeth Williams</a> and Rachel Banham. The rest of the rotation remains unsettled.</p><p>Long term, they also need to figure out the point guard spot. Neither Diggins, 35, nor Courtney Vandersloot, 37, will be around forever. Hailey Van Lith, the No. 11 pick in 2025, struggled to adapt as a rookie and is still recovering from an ankle injury.</p><p>The Sky added another option by trading for Jacy Sheldon. The No. 5 pick in 2024 draft bounced around her first two years — traded to Connecticut after her rookie season in Dallas, then dealt to Washington midway through 2025. But her stock climbed last season as she improved her 3-point percentage from 30.9 to 39.6.</p><p>Was that a fluke or the real deal? Can she keep developing into a high-quality starter?</p><p>The Sky gave up their 2028 first-round draft pick to find out.</p><p><b> Players Added</b><br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-fb4f68d0-35ee-11f1-b117-75b1bfb12b93"><li>Skylar Diggins, All-WNBA point guard</li><li>Azurá Stevens, 6’6 stretch big</li><li>Jacy Sheldon, 25-year old point guard</li><li>Elizabeth Williams, returner, defensive center</li><li>Rachel Banham, returner, shooter</li></ul><p><b>Draft Capital Retained (First-Round Only)</b><br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-fb4f68d1-35ee-11f1-b117-75b1bfb12b93"><li>2026 No. 5 pick</li><li>2027 pick</li><li>Dream’s 2027, 2028 picks (from Reese deal)</li></ul><p><b>Draft Capital Owed</b><br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-fb4f68d2-35ee-11f1-b117-75b1bfb12b93"><li>Mystics have the right to swap for the Sky’s 2027 pick</li><li>Sky sent the Mystics their 2028 pick (for Sheldon)</li></ul>
